text.title: Supplementary C4 Diagrams: Landscape, Dynamic, Deployment
text.summary: Three supplementary diagram types used alongside the core four levels: System Landscape (multi-system overview), Dynamic (runtime interaction for a specific use case), and Deployment (infrastructure mapping).

rule.statement: Create a System Landscape diagram when the organization owns multiple related systems that interact. Use it as an entry point before drilling into any individual System Context diagram.
rule.rationale: Large organizations operate dozens of systems that share users and data. The System Landscape provides the "Level 0" view — all systems and their broad relationships — so stakeholders can orient themselves before zooming into one system.

list.item: Elements: multiple software systems, users, key external systems
No technology details, no containers — this is the "birds-eye" view
When to create: 3+ internal systems with inter-system interactions

rule.statement: Create Dynamic diagrams to document the runtime interaction for one specific use case or user story. Use them to explain complex flows that are unclear from the static structure diagrams.
rule.rationale: Static diagrams (Context, Container, Component) show structure but not behavior. When a "place order" flow involves 7 services and a message broker, the static Container diagram cannot convey the sequence. Dynamic diagrams add time-ordered interaction without requiring full UML sequence diagram complexity.

list.item: Elements: actors (persons or systems), containers or components as boxes, numbered interaction arrows
Numbering: explicitly number steps to show sequence
One use case per diagram — do not combine multiple flows in one diagram
When to create: complex asynchronous flows, distributed transactions, multi-service fan-out/fan-in

rule.statement: Create Deployment diagrams to map containers to infrastructure. Show which containers run on which nodes (VMs, Kubernetes pods, serverless functions) and the deployment infrastructure topology. Create one diagram per environment (production, staging) when they differ.
rule.rationale: The Container diagram shows what to deploy; the Deployment diagram shows where it is deployed. Without a Deployment diagram, new team members cannot answer "what happens if this node fails?" or "which containers share a database server?".

list.item: Elements: deployment nodes (cloud regions, VMs, K8s clusters, pods), software system instances, container instances
Show redundancy: multiple instances, availability zones, load balancers
Label protocols and ports for firewall/security group planning
When to create: before infrastructure provisioning, when presenting disaster recovery design
